title: End warmly
concept: End a conversation with a clear, friendly close that respects time and avoids an abrupt disappearance.
skill_objective: User can leave a casual office or campus conversation with a warm close and a context-appropriate reason to go.

concept_intro.text: A good close does three small things: it signals that you valued the exchange, gives a simple true reason for leaving when one is useful, and ends clearly. You do not need a long apology or a promise to continue. A warm sentence lets both people move on without guessing whether the conversation has ended.

example.setting: In the office coffee corner, Maya has been chatting with her colleague Priya about local lunch spots. Maya needs to join a scheduled onboarding meeting.

dialogue.speaker: Maya
dialogue.text: That sandwich place sounds like a good find. Thanks for telling me about it.

dialogue.speaker: Maya
dialogue.text: I need to join my onboarding meeting now, but it was nice talking with you. See you around.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Of course—good luck with it. See you later.

bad.explanation: It is clear, but it drops the conversation without acknowledging the person or the exchange.

better.text: Sorry, I have a meeting. I really should go now, but maybe we can talk later, unless you are busy, and sorry again.
better.explanation: It gives a reason, but the repeated apology and uncertainty make a simple exit sound heavier than it needs to be.

best.text: Thanks for the lunch tip. I need to join my onboarding meeting now, but it was nice talking with you. See you around.
best.explanation: It signals appreciation, gives a brief context-appropriate reason, and ends cleanly. Priya can return the close without being asked to make a plan.

exercise.prompt: You are chatting in the coffee corner and your scheduled onboarding meeting is about to begin. Which exit is warm and clear?

options.feedback: This announces the exit but does not acknowledge the conversation or offer a friendly close.

options.text: I am so sorry, I should have left earlier, I do not want to be rude, maybe I can stay for another minute...
options.feedback: A long apology can make the departure feel awkward and delays the clean ending you need.

options.text: Thanks for telling me about that lunch spot. I need to join my onboarding meeting now, but it was nice talking with you. See you around.
options.feedback: Correct. It appreciates the exchange, gives a simple reason, and closes clearly without overexplaining.

practice.scenario_setup: At the office coffee corner, a colleague has just recommended a nearby lunch spot. Your scheduled onboarding meeting starts now.
practice.user_task: Draft a warm exit with appreciation, one brief reason, and a clean ending. Then compare it with an abrupt version and an overexplained version; keep the final version simple and in your own voice.

parts.question: Revise this abrupt close: “I have to go.” Which version signals appreciation, a reason, and a clean ending?

options.text: I have to go, but you should tell me every detail about the lunch places before I leave.
options.feedback: This adds a demand that keeps the conversation going instead of giving it a clean, respectful close.

options.text: Thanks for the recommendation. I need to join my onboarding meeting now, so I will see you around.
options.feedback: Correct. It appreciates the colleague's contribution, names the real reason to leave, and makes the ending unambiguous.

options.text: Sorry, sorry, I should not have started talking when I had a meeting, and I feel terrible about leaving.
options.feedback: This overexplains and turns an ordinary schedule change into something the colleague must reassure you about.

parts.question: Why is “Thanks for the recommendation. I need to join my onboarding meeting now, so I will see you around” a strong close?

options.text: It shows appreciation, gives a brief reason, and lets the conversation end without requiring either person to make another plan.
options.feedback: Correct. Those three elements make the exit friendly, proportionate, and easy to receive.

options.text: It guarantees that the colleague will want to continue the conversation later.
options.feedback: A warm close does not predict or require future contact; it simply ends the current exchange well.

options.text: It makes the colleague responsible for persuading you to stay.
options.feedback: A clear close does not ask the other person to manage your schedule or keep the interaction going.

parts.prompt: Revise this exit from a coffee-corner chat: “Okay, bye.” Include one genuine sign of appreciation, the scheduled meeting as a brief reason, and a clean final line. Do not add a promise or invitation you do not mean.
parts.good_answer_demonstrates: A short, warm close that acknowledges something from the exchange, names the onboarding meeting as a simple reason to leave, and clearly ends the conversation without overexplaining or making an unwanted commitment.
